informal: Prove that if $H$ has finite index $n$ then there is a normal subgroup $K$ of $G$ with $K \leq H$ and $|G: K| \leq n!$.
reasoning: The Lean signature captures the same claim: given a subgroup H of finite index n (the hypothesis n > 0 together with H.index = n rules out the Mathlib convention `index = 0` for infinite index, matching the informal "finite index n"), it asserts the existence of a subgroup K ≤ H that is normal in G with [G:K] ≤ n!. The quantifier structure (∃ K ≤ H, K.Normal ∧ K.index ≤ n!) matches "there is a normal subgroup K of G with K ≤ H and |G:K| ≤ n!". No extra hypotheses (e.g. finiteness of G, commutativity) are imposed, and no part of the conclusion is dropped or strengthened. Hence the formalization is logically equivalent to the informal statement.
